questions.question: Describe the difference between 'atomic commit protocol' and 'two-phase commit protocol'. Provide an example of when you would use each. (15 marks)
questions.hint: Atomic commit protocols are simpler and more efficient, but may not work in all scenarios. Two-phase commit protocols provide more guarantees, but may be slower.

questions.question: An example of a distributed deadlock is when two transactions, T1 and T2, are executed concurrently on two different sites. T1 locks site A, and T2 locks site B. T1 then requests to lock site C, but site C is locked by T2, which is waiting for T1 to release site B. Describe how to resolve this deadlock. (12 marks)
questions.hint: One way to resolve the deadlock is to use 'abort and retry'. This involves aborting one of the transactions and retrying it. Another approach is to use 'lock ordering', where transactions are given a fixed order in which to lock sites.

questions.question: A distributed transaction fails due to site failure. Describe the steps involved in recovering the transaction and ensuring the system remains in a consistent state. (15 marks)
questions.hint: First, the transaction manager may need to abort any transactions that have been partially committed. Then, the transaction manager may need to roll back any local database modifications. Finally, the transaction manager may need to commit any local database changes that were not affected by the failed transaction.
